solution: The integrand \( |x - ([[c]])| \) has its corner at \( x = [[c]] \), which lies in \( ([[a]], [[b]]) \). Split the integral there: \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[c]]}(([[c]]) - x)\,dx + \int_{[[c]]}^{[[b]]}(x - ([[c]]))\,dx = \frac{([[c]] - [[a]])^{2}}{2} + \frac{([[b]] - [[c]])^{2}}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the absolute value and integrates \( x - ([[c]]) \) directly, so the part below the axis cancels area it should add.
This counts only the piece to the right of the corner \( x = [[c]] \).
This counts only the piece to the left of the corner \( x = [[c]] \).
This treats \( |x - ([[c]])| \) as constant at its midpoint value times the width.
This squares the whole interval width, ignoring where the corner actually falls.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Evaluate \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} \left| x - ([[c]]) \right|\, dx \). (Note where the corner of the absolute value falls relative to the interval of integration.)
solution: The corner of \( |x - ([[c]])| \) is at \( x = [[c]] \), which lies OUTSIDE \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \) since \( [[c]] > [[b]] \). So \( x - ([[c]]) \) keeps one sign (negative) throughout the interval, and no splitting is needed: \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} \left(([[c]]) - x\right) dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly splits the integral at the corner as if it were inside the interval.
This treats the two endpoints as if they straddled the corner, subtracting squared distances instead of using the single-sign form.
This adds squared distances from both endpoints to the corner, as if there were two pieces.
This squares the interval width instead of using the single linear antiderivative.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: The integrand \( x^{2} - [[rsq]] \) has roots at \( x = \pm [[r]] \), and \( -[[w]] < -[[r]] < [[r]] < [[w]] \), so \( |x^{2}-[[rsq]]| \) has TWO corners. It is positive on \( [-[[w]],-[[r]]] \) and \( [[[r]],[[w]]] \) and negative (so the absolute value flips it) on \( [-[[r]],[[r]]] \). Splitting at both corners and using symmetry gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the absolute value and integrates \( x^{2} - [[rsq]] \) directly, so the negative middle piece cancels area it should add.
This treats the whole integrand as constant at its value at \( x=0 \) (namely \( [[rsq]] \)) times the interval width.
This ignores the \( -[[rsq]] \) shift and integrates \( x^{2} \) alone.
This uses \( r^{3} \) as if the middle piece contributed nothing, rather than flipping its sign.
This is off by 2 from the correct value.
This is off by 2 from the correct value.
This is off by 4 from the correct value.

solution: The sum \( |x-([[c1]])| + |x-([[c2]])| \) has TWO corners, at \( x=[[c1]] \) and \( x=[[c2]] \), splitting \( [[[lo]],[[hi]]] \) into three pieces on which each absolute value is single-signed. Splitting at both corners and antidifferentiating each piece gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the sum as if it were constant across the whole interval, squaring the total width.
This only accounts for the two outer pieces and drops the middle piece between the corners.
This uses the gap between the corners as a uniform 'height' times the full width, doubled.
This drops the absolute values and integrates \( (x-c_1)+(x-c_2) \) directly, letting negative contributions cancel.
This is off by 2 from the correct value.
This is off by 2 from the correct value.
This is off by 4 from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[m1]]x + [[b1]] \) and \( g(x) = [[m2]]x \). Evaluate \( \int_{[[lo]]}^{[[hi]]} \max\bigl(f(x), g(x)\bigr)\, dx \).
solution: The two lines cross where \( [[m1]]x + [[b1]] = [[m2]]x \), i.e. at \( x = [[k]] \), which lies inside \( [[[lo]],[[hi]]] \). On each side of that crossing point, \( \max(f,g) \) equals whichever line is on top there; splitting the integral at \( x=[[k]] \) and integrating each piece gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This integrates \( f \) alone over the whole interval, ignoring where \( g \) is actually larger.
This integrates \( g \) alone over the whole interval, ignoring where \( f \) is actually larger.
This averages the two functions instead of taking the pointwise maximum.
This adds the two functions instead of taking the pointwise maximum.
This is off by 2 from the correct value.
This is off by 2 from the correct value.
This is off by 4 from the correct value.

stem: A function is defined piecewise by \( f(x) = ([[a]])x + ([[b]]) \) for \( x < [[c]] \), and \( f(x) = ([[d]])x + ([[e]]) \) for \( x \ge [[c]] \). Evaluate \( \int_{[[lo]]}^{[[hi]]} f(x)\, dx \).
solution: Split the integral at the breakpoint \( x=[[c]] \), using the FIRST piece \( ([[a]])x+([[b]]) \) on \( [[[lo]],[[c]]] \) and the SECOND piece \( ([[d]])x+([[e]]) \) on \( [[[c]],[[hi]]] \): \( \int_{[[lo]]}^{[[c]]} \left(([[a]])x+([[b]])\right)dx + \int_{[[c]]}^{[[hi]]} \left(([[d]])x+([[e]])\right)dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses only the FIRST piece's formula across the whole interval, ignoring the breakpoint.
This uses only the SECOND piece's formula across the whole interval, ignoring the breakpoint.
This subtracts the second piece's contribution instead of adding it.
This swaps which formula applies on which side of the breakpoint.
This is off by 2 from the correct value.
This is off by 2 from the correct value.
This is off by 4 from the correct value.

solution: \( \sin(x) \) is nonnegative on \( [0,\pi] \) and nonpositive on \( [\pi,2\pi] \), so \( |\sin(x)| \) folds every half-period up. Each length-\( \pi \) piece contributes \( \int_{0}^{\pi} ([[a]])\sin(x)\,dx = 2([[a]]) \), and there are \( [[n]] \) such pieces in \( [0,[[n]]\pi] \), giving \( 2([[a]])([[n]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes the positive and negative half-periods cancel as they would WITHOUT the absolute value.
This drops the factor of 2 that comes from \( \int_0^\pi \sin(x)\,dx = 2 \).
This treats \( |\sin(x)| \) as if it averaged to \( 1 \) over each length-\( \pi \) piece instead of \( 2/\pi \).
This double-counts the already-doubled per-period contribution.
This is off by 2 from the correct value.
This is off by 2 from the correct value.
This halves the correct contribution per period.
